question:
If $p$ is a prime number greater than 3 and $k = \lfloor 2p/3
\rfloor$, prove that the sum
\[
\binom p1 + \binom p2 + \cdots + \binom pk
\]
of binomial coefficients is divisible by $p^2$.
solution:
For $1 \leq n \leq p-1$, $p$ divides $\binom pn$ and
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{p} \binom pn &= \frac{1}{n} \frac{p-1}{1} \frac{p-2}{2} \cdots
\frac{p-n+1}{n-1} \\
&\equiv \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} \mymod{p},
\end{align*}
where the congruence $x \equiv y \mymod{p}$ means that $x-y$ is a
rational number whose numerator, in reduced form, is divisible by $p$.
Hence it suffices to show that
\[
\sum_{n=1}^k \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} \equiv 0 \mymod{p}.
\]
We distinguish two cases based on $p \mymod{6}$. First suppose $p =
6r+1$, so that $k = 4r$. Then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{n=1}^{4r} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n}
&= \sum_{n=1}^{4r} \frac{1}{n} - 2 \sum_{n=1}^{2r} \frac{1}{2n} \\
&= \sum_{n=1}^{2r} \left( \frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{n} \right)
+ \sum_{n=2r+1}^{3r} \left( \frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{6r+1-n} \right) \\
&= \sum_{n=2r+1}^{3r} \frac{p}{n(p-n)} \equiv 0 \mymod{p},
\end{align*}
since $p = 6r+1$.

Now suppose $p = 6r+5$, so that $k = 4r + 3$. A similar argument gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{n=1}^{4r+3}\ \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n}
&= \sum_{n=1}^{4r+3} \frac{1}{n} + 2 \sum_{n=1}^{2r+1} \frac{1}{2n} \\
&= \sum_{n=1}^{2r+1} \left( \frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{n} \right)
+ \sum_{n=2r+2}^{3r+2} \left( \frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{6r+5-n} \right) \\
&= \sum_{n=2r+2}^{3r+2} \frac{p}{n(p-n)} \equiv 0 \mymod{p}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long.question:
If $primevalue$ is a prime number greater than 3 and $upperindex = \lfloor 2primevalue/3 \rfloor$, prove that the sum
\[
\binom{primevalue}{1} + \binom{primevalue}{2} + \cdots + \binom{primevalue}{upperindex}
\]
of binomial coefficients is divisible by $primevalue^2$.
descriptive_long.solution:
For $1 \leq counter \leq primevalue-1$, primevalue divides $\binom{primevalue}{counter}$ and
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{primevalue} \binom{primevalue}{counter} &= \frac{1}{counter} \frac{primevalue-1}{1} \frac{primevalue-2}{2} \cdots
\frac{primevalue-counter+1}{counter-1} \\
&\equiv \frac{(-1)^{counter-1}}{counter} \mymod{primevalue},
\end{align*}
where the congruence $variablex \equiv variabley \mymod{primevalue}$ means that $variablex-variabley$ is a
rational number whose numerator, in reduced form, is divisible by primevalue.
Hence it suffices to show that
\[
\sum_{counter=1}^{upperindex} \frac{(-1)^{counter-1}}{counter} \equiv 0 \mymod{primevalue}.
\]
We distinguish two cases based on $primevalue \mymod{6}$. First suppose $primevalue =
6auxfactor+1$, so that $upperindex = 4auxfactor$. Then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{counter=1}^{4auxfactor} \frac{(-1)^{counter-1}}{counter}
&= \sum_{counter=1}^{4auxfactor} \frac{1}{counter} - 2 \sum_{counter=1}^{2auxfactor} \frac{1}{2counter} \\
&= \sum_{counter=1}^{2auxfactor} \left( \frac{1}{counter} - \frac{1}{counter} \right)
+ \sum_{counter=2auxfactor+1}^{3auxfactor} \left( \frac{1}{counter} + \frac{1}{6auxfactor+1-counter} \right) \\
&= \sum_{counter=2auxfactor+1}^{3auxfactor} \frac{primevalue}{counter(primevalue-counter)} \equiv 0 \mymod{primevalue},
\end{align*}
since $primevalue = 6auxfactor+1$.

Now suppose $primevalue = 6auxfactor+5$, so that $upperindex = 4auxfactor + 3$. A similar argument gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{counter=1}^{4auxfactor+3}\ \frac{(-1)^{counter-1}}{counter}
&= \sum_{counter=1}^{4auxfactor+3} \frac{1}{counter} + 2 \sum_{counter=1}^{2auxfactor+1} \frac{1}{2counter} \\
&= \sum_{counter=1}^{2auxfactor+1} \left( \frac{1}{counter} - \frac{1}{counter} \right)
+ \sum_{counter=2auxfactor+2}^{3auxfactor+2} \left( \frac{1}{counter} + \frac{1}{6auxfactor+5-counter} \right) \\
&= \sum_{counter=2auxfactor+2}^{3auxfactor+2} \frac{primevalue}{counter(primevalue-counter)} \equiv 0 \mymod{primevalue}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
If $chandelier$ is a prime number greater than 3 and $snowflake = \lfloor 2chandelier/3\rfloor$, prove that the sum
\[
\binom chandelier1 + \binom chandelier2 + \cdots + \binom chandeliersnowflake
\]
of binomial coefficients is divisible by $chandelier^2$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
For $1 \leq sunflower \leq chandelier-1$, $chandelier$ divides $\binom chandeliersunflower$ and
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{chandelier} \binom chandeliersunflower &= \frac{1}{sunflower} \frac{chandelier-1}{1} \frac{chandelier-2}{2} \cdots
\frac{chandelier-sunflower+1}{sunflower-1} \\
&\equiv \frac{(-1)^{sunflower-1}}{sunflower} \mymod{chandelier},
\end{align*}
where the congruence $marigold \equiv daffodil \mymod{chandelier}$ means that $marigold-daffodil$ is a
rational number whose numerator, in reduced form, is divisible by $chandelier$.
Hence it suffices to show that
\[
\sum_{sunflower=1}^{snowflake} \frac{(-1)^{sunflower-1}}{sunflower} \equiv 0 \mymod{chandelier}.
\]
We distinguish two cases based on $chandelier \mymod{6}$. First suppose $chandelier =
6tangerine+1$, so that $snowflake = 4tangerine$. Then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{sunflower=1}^{4tangerine} \frac{(-1)^{sunflower-1}}{sunflower}
&= \sum_{sunflower=1}^{4tangerine} \frac{1}{sunflower} - 2 \sum_{sunflower=1}^{2tangerine} \frac{1}{2sunflower} \\
&= \sum_{sunflower=1}^{2tangerine} \left( \frac{1}{sunflower} - \frac{1}{sunflower} \right)
+ \sum_{sunflower=2tangerine+1}^{3tangerine} \left( \frac{1}{sunflower} + \frac{1}{6tangerine+1-sunflower} \right) \\
&= \sum_{sunflower=2tangerine+1}^{3tangerine} \frac{chandelier}{sunflower(chandelier-sunflower)} \equiv 0 \mymod{chandelier},
\end{align*}
since $chandelier = 6tangerine+1$.

Now suppose $chandelier = 6tangerine+5$, so that $snowflake = 4tangerine + 3$. A similar argument gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{sunflower=1}^{4tangerine+3}\ \frac{(-1)^{sunflower-1}}{sunflower}
&= \sum_{sunflower=1}^{4tangerine+3} \frac{1}{sunflower} + 2 \sum_{sunflower=1}^{2tangerine+1} \frac{1}{2sunflower} \\
&= \sum_{sunflower=1}^{2tangerine+1} \left( \frac{1}{sunflower} - \frac{1}{sunflower} \right)
+ \sum_{sunflower=2tangerine+2}^{3tangerine+2} \left( \frac{1}{sunflower} + \frac{1}{6tangerine+5-sunflower} \right) \\
&= \sum_{sunflower=2tangerine+2}^{3tangerine+2} \frac{chandelier}{sunflower(chandelier-sunflower)} \equiv 0 \mymod{chandelier}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
If $composite$ is a prime number greater than 3 and $ceilingvalue = \lfloor 2composite/3 \rfloor$, prove that the sum
\[
\binom{composite}{1} + \binom{composite}{2} + \cdots + \binom{composite}{ceilingvalue}
\]
of binomial coefficients is divisible by $composite^2$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
For $1 \leq continuous \leq composite-1$, $composite$ divides $\binom{composite}{continuous}$ and
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{composite} \binom{composite}{continuous} &= \frac{1}{continuous} \frac{composite-1}{1} \frac{composite-2}{2} \cdots
\frac{composite-continuous+1}{continuous-1} \\
&\equiv \frac{(-1)^{continuous-1}}{continuous} \mymod{composite},
\end{align*}
where the congruence $fixedvalue \equiv horizontal \mymod{composite}$ means that $fixedvalue-horizontal$ is a
rational number whose numerator, in reduced form, is divisible by $composite$.
Hence it suffices to show that
\[
\sum_{continuous=1}^{ceilingvalue} \frac{(-1)^{continuous-1}}{continuous} \equiv 0 \mymod{composite}.
\]
We distinguish two cases based on $composite \mymod{6}$. First suppose $composite = 6multiple+1$, so that $ceilingvalue = 4multiple$. Then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{continuous=1}^{4multiple} \frac{(-1)^{continuous-1}}{continuous}
&= \sum_{continuous=1}^{4multiple} \frac{1}{continuous} - 2 \sum_{continuous=1}^{2multiple} \frac{1}{2continuous} \\
&= \sum_{continuous=1}^{2multiple} \left( \frac{1}{continuous} - \frac{1}{continuous} \right)
+ \sum_{continuous=2multiple+1}^{3multiple} \left( \frac{1}{continuous} + \frac{1}{6multiple+1-continuous} \right) \\
&= \sum_{continuous=2multiple+1}^{3multiple} \frac{composite}{continuous(composite-continuous)} \equiv 0 \mymod{composite},
\end{align*}
since $composite = 6multiple+1$.

Now suppose $composite = 6multiple+5$, so that $ceilingvalue = 4multiple + 3$. A similar argument gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{continuous=1}^{4multiple+3}\ \frac{(-1)^{continuous-1}}{continuous}
&= \sum_{continuous=1}^{4multiple+3} \frac{1}{continuous} + 2 \sum_{continuous=1}^{2multiple+1} \frac{1}{2continuous} \\
&= \sum_{continuous=1}^{2multiple+1} \left( \frac{1}{continuous} - \frac{1}{continuous} \right)
+ \sum_{continuous=2multiple+2}^{3multiple+2} \left( \frac{1}{continuous} + \frac{1}{6multiple+5-continuous} \right) \\
&= \sum_{continuous=2multiple+2}^{3multiple+2} \frac{composite}{continuous(composite-continuous)} \equiv 0 \mymod{composite}.
\end{align*}

garbled_string.question:
If $ftbzcxao$ is a prime number greater than 3 and $rwldnuye = \lfloor 2ftbzcxao/3\rfloor$, prove that the sum
\[
\binom {ftbzcxao}1 + \binom {ftbzcxao}2 + \cdots + \binom {ftbzcxao}{rwldnuye}
\]
of binomial coefficients is divisible by $ftbzcxao^2$.
garbled_string.solution:
For $1 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq ftbzcxao-1$, $ftbzcxao$ divides $\binom {ftbzcxao}{qzxwvtnp}$ and
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{ftbzcxao} \binom {ftbzcxao}{qzxwvtnp} &= \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \frac{ftbzcxao-1}{1} \frac{ftbzcxao-2}{2} \cdots
\frac{ftbzcxao-qzxwvtnp+1}{qzxwvtnp-1} \\
&\equiv \frac{(-1)^{qzxwvtnp-1}}{qzxwvtnp} \mymod{ftbzcxao},
\end{align*}
where the congruence $hjgrksla \equiv mplkzqsn \mymod{ftbzcxao}$ means that $hjgrksla-mplkzqsn$ is a
rational number whose numerator, in reduced form, is divisible by $ftbzcxao$.
Hence it suffices to show that
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{rwldnuye} \frac{(-1)^{qzxwvtnp-1}}{qzxwvtnp} \equiv 0 \mymod{ftbzcxao}.
\]
We distinguish two cases based on $ftbzcxao \mymod{6}$. First suppose $ftbzcxao =
6gacplmse+1$, so that $rwldnuye = 4gacplmse$. Then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{4gacplmse} \frac{(-1)^{qzxwvtnp-1}}{qzxwvtnp}
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{4gacplmse} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} - 2 \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{2gacplmse} \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp} \\
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{2gacplmse} \left( \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} - \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \right)
+ \sum_{qzxwvtnp=2gacplmse+1}^{3gacplmse} \left( \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} + \frac{1}{6gacplmse+1-qzxwvtnp} \right) \\
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=2gacplmse+1}^{3gacplmse} \frac{ftbzcxao}{qzxwvtnp(ftbzcxao-qzxwvtnp)} \equiv 0 \mymod{ftbzcxao},
\end{align*}
since $ftbzcxao = 6gacplmse+1$.

Now suppose $ftbzcxao = 6gacplmse+5$, so that $rwldnuye = 4gacplmse + 3$. A similar argument gives
\begin{align*}
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{4gacplmse+3}\ \frac{(-1)^{qzxwvtnp-1}}{qzxwvtnp}
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{4gacplmse+3} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} + 2 \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{2gacplmse+1} \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp} \\
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{2gacplmse+1} \left( \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} - \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \right)
+ \sum_{qzxwvtnp=2gacplmse+2}^{3gacplmse+2} \left( \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} + \frac{1}{6gacplmse+5-qzxwvtnp} \right) \\
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=2gacplmse+2}^{3gacplmse+2} \frac{ftbzcxao}{qzxwvtnp(ftbzcxao-qzxwvtnp)} \equiv 0 \mymod{ftbzcxao}.
\end{align*}

kernel_variant.question:
Let p be a prime number strictly larger than 3 and set

                  k = \lfloor  2p/3 \rfloor  .

Let
                  A = 1/k \cdot  ( C(p,1) + C(p,2) + \ldots  + C(p,k) ) ,

where C(p,n) = \binom{p}{n}.  (Thus A is the arithmetic mean of the first k non-trivial binomial coefficients of order p.)

Show that when A is written in lowest terms its numerator is divisible by p^2.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write x \equiv _p y to mean that x - y is a rational number whose
reduced numerator is a multiple of p.  (In particular, x \equiv _p 0 says that the
reduced numerator of x is divisible by p.)

Step 1 - Removing one factor p.
For 1 \leq  n \leq  p - 1 the prime p divides \binom{p}{n} and

    1/p \cdot  \binom{p}{n}
     = 1/n \cdot  (p-1)/1 \cdot  (p-2)/2 \cdot  \ldots  \cdot  (p-n+1)/(n-1)
     \equiv _p (-1)^{n-1} / n .

Multiplying the definition of A by 1/p and using this congruence we obtain

        A / p \equiv _p (1/k) \cdot  \Sigma _{n=1}^{k} (-1)^{n-1} / n .

Hence A will be divisible by p^2 once we prove

        S := \Sigma _{n=1}^{k} (-1)^{n-1} / n  \equiv _p 0.      (1)

Step 2 - Even and odd denominators.
Write the prime either as p = 3q + 1 or p = 3q + 2 (q \geq  1 because p > 3).
Then
           k = \lfloor  2p/3 \rfloor  = 2q          if  p = 3q + 1 ,
           k = \lfloor  2p/3 \rfloor  = 2q + 1      if  p = 3q + 2 .

Separate the terms of S according to the parity of the denominator:

        S = \Sigma _{n=1}^{k} 1/n - 2 \Sigma _{n=1}^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor } 1/(2n) .      (2)

Step 3 - Rewriting S as a harmonic tail.

*  If k = 2q (i.e. p = 3q + 1), then \lfloor k/2\rfloor  = q and (2) gives
        S = \Sigma _{n=1}^{2q} 1/n - 2 \Sigma _{n=1}^{q} 1/(2n)
          = \Sigma _{n=q+1}^{2q} 1/n .                              (3)

*  If k = 2q + 1 (i.e. p = 3q + 2), then \lfloor k/2\rfloor  = q and
        S = \Sigma _{n=1}^{2q+1} 1/n - 2 \Sigma _{n=1}^{q} 1/(2n)
          = \Sigma _{n=q+1}^{2q+1} 1/n .                            (4)

Thus in either case
        S = \Sigma _{n=q+1}^{m} 1/n ,
where m = 2q or m = 2q + 1 respectively.

Step 4 - Using the symmetry n \leftrightarrow  p - n (corrected counting).
Because p = 3q + 1 or 3q + 2, the set
        R := { n \in  \mathbb{Z} : q < n \leq  m }                 (so q < n \leq  2q   or   q < n \leq  2q+1)
obeys the symmetry n \in  R  \Leftrightarrow   p - n \in  R ; moreover exactly one of the two
integers in the pair { n , p - n } lies below p/2 while the other lies above
p/2.  Consequently every unordered pair { n , p - n } contributes once (and
only once) to the next sum when we restrict ourselves to the elements that
are < p/2.  Hence

        S = \Sigma _{ q < n < p/2 } ( 1/n + 1/(p - n) ).            (5)

(No factor 1/2 is present, because the restriction n < p/2 removes the
duplication.)

Each summand in (5) equals
        1/n + 1/(p - n) = p / [ n(p - n) ] ,
whose reduced numerator is a multiple of p.  Therefore every term of (5) is
\equiv _p 0, and so
        S \equiv _p 0 .

Step 5 - Restoring the missing factor of p.
We have shown S \equiv _p 0, whence A/p \equiv _p 0.  The reduced numerator of A/p is
therefore divisible by p; multiplying by the factor p that we removed at the
start shows that the reduced numerator of A is divisible by p^2.

This completes the proof that the arithmetic mean A is divisible by p^2.

core_steps: Use p | C(p,n) to rewrite (1/p)·C(p,n) ≡ (−1)^{n−1}/n (mod p).
Reduce the problem to showing  S = Σ_{n=1}^{k} (−1)^{n−1}/n ≡ 0 (mod p).
Write the prime as 3q+1 or 3q+2 (equivalently 6r+1 or 6r+5), giving k = 2q or 2q+1, and split S into odd–even harmonic parts.
Pair terms n and p−n so that each pair gives p/[n(p−n)], which is 0 modulo p.
Hence S ≡ 0 (mod p) ⇒ original binomial sum ≡ 0 (mod p²).

slot1.description: Notation used for congruence modulo p.

slot2.description: Choice of modulus-6 parametrisation (6r+1 / 6r+5, k=4r or 4r+3); one could equally use modulus 3 (3q+1 / 3q+2, k=2q or 2q+1) without affecting the argument.
